segments.title: The Continental System and Britain's Baltic stake
segments.summary: The opening context links the 1807 expedition to Napoleon's Continental System and Britain's dependence on Baltic supplies and North Sea trade.
segments.body: Dr. Clarke sets the Second Battle of Copenhagen beside the 1801 action, then explains why the 1807 crisis mattered more than a dispute with Denmark alone. Napoleon's Continental System sought to exclude Britain from European commerce. Britain treated Baltic stores and the trade routes around the North Sea as vital economic and naval resources. Denmark's apparent movement into that system therefore raised the prospect that a strategically located fleet and coastline could serve a hostile continental order.

evidence.note: The transcript contrasts 1801 and 1807 and identifies the Continental System, Baltic stores, and North Sea trade as Britain's concern.

segments.title: Parker's signal and Nelson's blind eye
segments.summary: The familiar story of Nelson ignoring Parker's signal is placed within the command problem of the 1801 battle and the capture of Danish ships.
segments.body: The video briefly returns to the First Battle of Copenhagen to explain the command relationship behind Nelson's celebrated gesture. Admiral Hyde Parker held overall command because Nelson's conduct around Lady Hamilton had damaged confidence in him. Dr. Clarke suggests Parker's signal may have given Nelson discretion to withdraw if necessary, while Nelson famously claimed not to see it through his blind eye. The episode ends with the British forcing the harbour and capturing Danish ships, establishing the precedent that made the renewed 1807 vulnerability so striking.

evidence.note: The transcript explains Parker's command, the signal that may have allowed withdrawal, Nelson's blind-eye response, and the capture of Danish ships.

segments.title: An unready Danish fleet concentrated at Copenhagen
segments.summary: Denmark repeated the central vulnerability of 1801 by leaving an unready fleet concentrated in its capital with too few defenders to deter a stronger British expedition.
segments.body: Six years after the first attack, Anglo-Danish communications again broke down as Denmark moved toward the French-led system. Copenhagen held only about 5,000 regular troops and a similar number of militia. The Danish fleet was concentrated in the capital, unready for sea, and smaller than the force Britain could deploy against it. Dr. Clarke treats that unchanged posture as the immediate operational weakness that allowed the same coercive method to work twice.

evidence.note: The transcript describes the diplomatic breakdown, the limited Copenhagen garrison, and a concentrated Danish fleet that was neither ready for sea nor equal to the coming British force.

segments.title: The Dardanelles lesson: adapt after the first attack
segments.summary: A comparison with repeated attempts to force the Dardanelles turns Copenhagen into a broader lesson about learning, fortification, and denying an enemy the same success twice.
segments.body: Dr. Clarke contrasts Denmark's repeated exposure with the defensive learning visible at the Dardanelles. Successive passages or attacks taught the Ottoman defenders how to make later attempts more difficult, and the World War I experience reinforced the value of adapting the defences. His wider point is that states usually change their preparations after an enemy demonstrates a successful method. Copenhagen stands out because Britain could return six years later with essentially the same strategic purpose and again find the Danish fleet vulnerable in the capital.

evidence.note: The transcript uses repeated attempts at the Dardanelles to argue that defenders normally learn from an attack and deny the same tactics another easy success.

segments.title: Denmark's strategic bind and its naval consequences
segments.summary: Denmark faced a genuine choice between French invasion by land and British attack by sea, but alignment with Napoleon required naval preparations that Copenhagen had not made.
segments.body: The video treats Denmark's alignment problem as a real strategic dilemma rather than simple incompetence. Refusing Napoleon while sharing a land frontier with him carried a grave risk of invasion, so accommodation with France could appear to be the safer course. Yet that choice made Royal Navy intervention predictable because Denmark was a seafaring state positioned near routes Britain considered vital. Dr. Clarke's practical lesson is that a maritime state expecting British hostility must either prepare to defend its fleet or give it room to operate at sea.

evidence.note: The transcript weighs the danger of refusing Napoleon against the predictable Royal Navy response and argues that Denmark needed either to defend its navy or put it to sea.

segments.title: What it meant to “Copenhagen” an enemy
segments.summary: Dr. Clarke explains the episode's enduring shorthand: combining surprise with the seizure of an opponent's means of resistance.
segments.body: The Second Battle of Copenhagen gave rise, in the video's telling, to the verb “to Copenhagen”: to surprise an opponent and take everything significant from them. The phrase captures the thoroughness of the seizure as well as the advantage of surprise. The 1807 operation differed from 1801 by pairing the naval force with substantial troops ashore. That combination makes the phrase a useful entry point into pre-emptive maritime coercion rather than a simple fleet battle.

evidence.note: The transcript defines the term and contrasts the 1807 use of ground forces with the first battle.

segments.title: Narrow waters, shore guns, and mortar ships
segments.summary: A tactical comparison with 1801 shows why Copenhagen's channels and fortifications should have favored a prepared defence—and how British mortar ships helped overcome it.
segments.body: Dr. Clarke stresses that forcing a fleet through narrow channels ought to expose it to concentrated fire from prepared shore positions. Land batteries could mount weapons without the same size constraints as a ship and employ heated shot against wooden hulls. British mortar vessels answered that advantage with specialist guns operated through Royal Artillery expertise as well as naval gunnery knowledge. Concentrating a large pool of mathematical and gunnery skill on only one or two mortars made those vessels unusually accurate. Their bombardment helped suppress or push back defences so that Nelson's force could close in and capture Danish ships.

evidence.note: The transcript discusses defended channels, heated shot, mortar vessels, artillery specialists, and the approach that enabled Nelson's manoeuvre.

segments.title: Gambier and Cathcart's joint command team
segments.summary: The expedition's naval and military leadership reveals that Copenhagen was planned as a combined operation, not merely a Royal Navy raid.
segments.body: The video introduces Admiral James Gambier and General William Cathcart as the commanders chosen for the 1807 expedition. Gambier is presented as an experienced admiral and an educational benefactor whose name survives at Gambier, Ohio. His later controversy at the Basque Roads arose when critics wanted him to pursue French ships onto dangerous rocks for possible prize money, while he judged the additional ships and lives at risk unnecessary. Dr. Clarke treats that dispute as entangled with political rivalry and defends Gambier as a capable commander. The Gambier-Cathcart pairing matters because the coming operation needed coordinated sea power, troops, and political direction rather than an independent naval action.

evidence.note: The transcript names Gambier and Cathcart, discusses Gambier's experience, and introduces their roles in the second battle.

segments.title: Cathcart's army and the weight of the expedition
segments.summary: Cathcart's force included senior officers, the King's German Legion, artillery, engineers, and a large siege train—evidence of a major combined expedition.
segments.body: Dr. Clarke argues that Cathcart deserves greater attention for both his diplomatic work with Russia and his command responsibilities in 1807. His later service as ambassador and military commissioner helped maintain the alliance against Napoleon during the wars of liberation. At Copenhagen, Cathcart commanded senior lieutenant generals and major generals, including Arthur Wellesley, whose reserve would do much of the fighting. The army included the King's German Legion, Guards and other brigades, engineers, 84 field guns, and 101 siege guns. That scale shows that British pressure on Copenhagen could be applied from land as well as from the sea.

evidence.note: The transcript covers Cathcart's wider career, the expedition's divisions, the King's German Legion, artillery, engineers, and the stated field and siege guns.

segments.title: Why Gambier chose a fleet of third rates
segments.summary: The fleet list explains why the Royal Navy relied chiefly on capable third rates rather than treating the expedition as a first-rate show of force.
segments.body: Gambier's initial force included the 98-gun Prince of Wales but was otherwise built around useful third rates, including both 74- and 64-gun ships. Dr. Clarke emphasizes that this was not a weak force: ships such as Captain, Goliath, Hercules, Orion, Resolution, Spencer, and Vanguard were solid fighting units. Gambier was senior enough to request first rates but deliberately left those scarce ships available for other operations and fleets. His vice admiral likewise received a third-rate flagship rather than a prestige vessel. The choice shows how a fleet commander could balance concentrated combat power at Copenhagen against the wider demands on a global navy.

evidence.note: The transcript identifies Prince of Wales, the third-rate-heavy force, several named ships, and Gambier's willingness to leave first rates for other operations.

segments.title: A second-rate flagship chosen for command
segments.summary: Gambier's choice of the second-rate Prince of Wales shows a commander valuing visibility and command organization over the status of a first-rate flagship.
segments.body: The video explains the trade-off in a second rate: considerable strength and height, but less graceful handling than longer first or third rates. Prince of Wales carried 98 guns, yet Gambier did not regard its modest broadside advantage over a 74 as the decisive benefit. He chose the second rate for the height of its masts, which improved his ability to see and direct a large operation. Gambier also preferred commodores to additional rear admirals at the outset. His unusual two-captain arrangement separated ship handling from flag-officer administration, giving the command team more capacity to organize the fleet.

evidence.note: The transcript compares second rates with other rates, explains Gambier's preference for mast height, and describes the two-captain arrangement.

segments.title: Reinforcements, shallow waters, and naval diplomacy
segments.summary: Later reinforcements strengthened Gambier's force, but the campaign still favored versatile ships that could operate toward Copenhagen's shallower approaches and compel negotiation.
segments.body: Additional 74s and a rear admiral joined the expedition, confirming the importance London attached to the operation. The reinforcements were capable ships with experienced captains, even though they did not represent the Royal Navy's most prestigious first-rate concentration. Dr. Clarke presents the preference for 74s and other third rates as an operational choice: they were strong enough to meet any likely opponent and more useful toward shallow waters than the largest ships. Cathcart and the fleet assembled during August, remaining visibly available before major action began in September. That display functioned as naval diplomacy, intended to make Denmark reconsider before coercion became unavoidable, but it failed to produce the desired settlement.

evidence.note: The transcript lists arriving reinforcements, explains the utility of third rates and Prince of Wales, and describes the force as an attempt at naval diplomacy.
